      "page_ref": "p.142",
      "raw_quote": "conjuring the ‘princes of the thumb’ by tracing a circle in the earth with a black-handled knife"

      "attributes": {"role": "demon king", "appears in nail-gazing"},
      "page_ref": "p.142",
      "raw_quote": "I adjure you, princes of the nail … that you should bring the king Minon in this nail, and the queen shall also come with him"

      "type": "spirit",
      "attributes": {"role": "benign spirit", "function": "reveals treasure in dream"},
      "page_ref": "p.140",
      "raw_quote": "on the following night Haram, a benign spirit, may come to me in my sleep and enkindle my heart and my mind"

      "attributes": {"role": "demon prince", "appears inscribed on knife handle"},

      "page_ref": "p.140",
      "raw_quote": "Three times he conjures the names themselves, that they may send the angels Michael, Gabriel and Raphael"

      "page_ref": "p.135–140",

      "raw_quote": "send wherever he will for a ram to be skinned and cooked, and then he should have a table set… have the ram set before him"

      "attributes": {"function": "protected ritual space"},
      "page_ref": "p.136",

      "attributes": {"function": "medium for spirit manifestation"},
      "page_ref": "p.135–140",

      "attributes": {"function": "reflective surface for spirit manifestation"},
      "page_ref": "p.138–139",

  "summary": "This chunk details several divinatory experiments from the Munich Handbook (CLM 849), focusing on nail-gazing, bone reflection, and basin divination using child mediums. It identifies named spirits such as Apolin, Maraloth, Berith, Astaroth, Minon, and Haram, describes ritual tools like black-handled knives and olive oil, outlines protective circles, and notes Jewish/Babylonian parallels in technique and demonology.",
